items.subtopic: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation | THE HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL
items.title: Why Hematopoietic Stem Cells (HSCs) are Clinically Feasible for Transplantation
items.content: The clinical feasibility of hematopoietic stem cell transplantation is attributed to several key features of HSCs. These include their remarkable regenerative capacity, ability to home to the marrow space after intravenous injection, and the capability to be cryopreserved without significant damage.
keyPoints: A single HSC can replace an entire lymphohematopoietic system in an adult mouse.
In humans, a small percentage of donor bone marrow volume results in complete replacement of the recipient's lymphohematopoietic system.
Homing involves interactions between P- and E-selectins on marrow sinusoidal endothelium with integrins like VLA-4 on HSCs.
CXCL12 from niche stroma interacts with CXCR4 on HSCs to facilitate migration to the stem cell niche.
Human HSCs can survive freezing and thawing, allowing for storage and later reinfusion.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation | THE HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL

items.subtopic: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation | THE HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL
items.title: How Hematopoietic Stem Cells (HSCs) Home to the Marrow Niche
items.content: The process of homing involves specific molecular interactions that guide HSCs from the bloodstream to their niche in the bone marrow. This is crucial for successful transplantation as it ensures the replacement and regeneration of the lymphohematopoietic system.
keyPoints: Homing begins with P- and E-selectins on marrow sinusoidal endothelium interacting with VLA-4 integrins on HSCs.
Tight adhesion occurs after changes in integrin conformation, allowing stem cells to migrate through the endothelium and extracellular matrix.
CXCL12 from niche stroma binds to CXCR4 on HSCs, facilitating their final migration to the stem cell niche.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation | THE HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL

items.title: Syngeneic Transplantation: Eliminating GVHD and Tumor Contamination
items.content: Syngeneic transplantation, using identical twins as donors, eliminates the risk of graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) and avoids contamination with tumor cells in the stem cell product.
keyPoints: No risk of GVHD with syngeneic donors.
No risk of tumor cell contamination unlike autologous marrow.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.title: HLA Matching and Its Impact on Allogeneic Transplantation
items.content: The degree of HLA matching between donor and recipient significantly influences the risks of graft rejection and GVHD in allogeneic transplantation.
keyPoints: Risk of graft rejection is 1–3% with HLA-identical siblings.
Severe acute GVHD risk is ~15% following transplantation between HLA-identical siblings.
Probability that a patient has an HLA-identical sibling is $1 - (0.75)^n$, where n equals the number of siblings.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.title: Autologous Transplantation: Benefits and Limitations
items.content: Autologous transplantation avoids GVHD and graft rejection but lacks a graft-versus-tumor effect and may involve tumor cell contamination.
keyPoints: No risk of GVHD or graft rejection in autologous transplantation.
Lacks graft-versus-tumor (GVT) effect.
Potential for tumor cell contamination leading to relapse.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.title: Peripheral Blood Stem Cell Mobilization and Collection
items.content: Hematopoietic progenitor cells can be mobilized into peripheral blood using myeloid growth factors like G-CSF, allowing for collection through pheresis. This method is preferred due to the ease of harvesting compared to bone marrow.
keyPoints: G-CSF administration increases CD34+ cell concentration in blood.
Typically 4-5 days of growth factor treatment followed by one or two 4-hour pheresis sessions are used for collection.
>2.5 × 10^6 CD34 cells/kg leads to rapid and sustained engraftment in autologous transplants.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.title: Advantages and Disadvantages of Peripheral Blood vs. Bone Marrow in Allogeneic Transplantation
items.content: Peripheral blood stem cells lead to faster engraftment than bone marrow but increase the risk of chronic GVHD due to donor T-cell contamination, affecting outcomes differently based on donor type.
keyPoints: Faster hematopoietic recovery with peripheral blood reduces morbidity but does not improve survival compared to marrow.
With matched sibling donors, faster engraftment and reduced relapse rates outweigh increased chronic GVHD risk.
In unmatched related transplants, peripheral blood increases chronic GVHD without improving survival.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.title: Use of Umbilical Cord Blood in Transplantation
items.content: Umbilical cord blood is a viable stem cell source with rapid availability and lower GVHD incidence, suitable for partially matched transplants.
keyPoints: Contains high concentrations of hematopoietic progenitor cells.
>800,000 units cryopreserved and available for use.
Lower T-cell content results in reduced GVHD risk.
Double cord transplants reduce graft failure risks compared to single cord units.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.title: Why and How Preparative Regimens Vary by Disease and Graft Source
items.content: The preparative regimen preceding hematopoietic cell transplantation is tailored based on the underlying disease and graft source. For severe combined immunodeficiency with a histocompatible sibling donor, no regimen is needed due to lack of host cells requiring eradication and inherent immune incompetence preventing rejection. Conversely, for aplastic anemia, high-dose cyclophosphamide plus antithymocyte globulin suffice for immunosuppression without needing extensive cell eradication.
keyPoints: No preparative regimen needed for severe combined immunodeficiency with histocompatible sibling donor due to lack of host cells and immune incompetence.
For aplastic anemia, high-dose cyclophosphamide plus antithymocyte globulin are sufficient for immunosuppression.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · THE TRANSPLANT PREPARATIVE REGIMEN

items.title: Reduced-Intensity vs. High-Dose Regimens in Transplantation
items.content: High-dose regimens, initially used for malignancies, have evolved to include reduced-intensity and nonmyeloablative options due to the immunologically mediated graft-versus-tumor (GVT) effect. Reduced-intensity regimens, such as fludarabine plus melphalan, offer less toxicity with lower transplant-related mortality but higher relapse rates compared to high-dose regimens. Nonmyeloablative regimens, like fludarabine plus 200 cGy total-body irradiation, cause transient myelosuppression and are suitable for older patients or those with comorbidities.
keyPoints: Reduced-intensity regimens (e.g., fludarabine plus melphalan) result in less toxicity but higher relapse rates than high-dose regimens.
Nonmyeloablative regimens (e.g., fludarabine plus 200 cGy total-body irradiation) cause transient myelosuppression and are suitable for older patients or those with comorbidities.
High-dose regimens are preferred for patients able to tolerate them, especially if there is measurable disease at transplantation.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · THE TRANSPLANT PREPARATIVE REGIMEN

items.title: Marrow Collection and Processing in Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation
items.content: During hematopoietic cell transplantation, marrow is typically collected from the donor's iliac crests under anesthesia. The aspirated marrow undergoes processing to remove fat and bony spicules using 0.3- and 0.2-mm screens. Depending on clinical needs, further processing may include red cell removal for ABO-incompatible transplants or T-cell removal to prevent GVHD.
keyPoints: Marrow collection is from the donor's posterior (and sometimes anterior) iliac crests under general or spinal anesthesia.
Typically 10–15 mL/kg of marrow is aspirated and placed in heparinized media.
Processing includes filtration through 0.3- and 0.2-mm screens to remove fat and bony spicules.
Further processing may involve red cell removal for ABO-incompatible transplants or T-cell removal to prevent GVHD.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · THE TRANSPLANT PROCEDURE

items.title: Stem Cell Collection and Infusion in Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation
items.content: Peripheral blood stem cells are collected via leukapheresis after donor treatment with hematopoietic growth factors, or chemotherapy plus growth factors for autologous transplants. These stem cells are infused through a central venous catheter and are generally well tolerated, though some patients may experience transient symptoms like fever or nausea.
keyPoints: Peripheral blood stem cells collected by leukapheresis after donor treatment with hematopoietic growth factors.
In autologous transplants, sometimes chemotherapy plus growth factors are used before collection.
Stem cell infusion is through a large-bore central venous catheter and usually well tolerated.
Possible transient symptoms include fever, cough, shortness of breath, or nausea/vomiting from cryoprotectant taste.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · THE TRANSPLANT PROCEDURE

items.title: Preventing Hemorrhagic Cystitis with High-Dose Cyclophosphamide
items.content: High-dose cyclophosphamide, used in preparative regimens for hematopoietic cell transplantation, can lead to hemorrhagic cystitis. This complication is preventable by employing bladder irrigation or administering the sulfhydryl compound mercaptoethanesulfonate (MESNA).
keyPoints: Hemorrhagic cystitis prevention with MESNA or bladder irrigation.
High-dose cyclophosphamide as a risk factor.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.title: Management of Sinusoidal Obstruction Syndrome
items.content: Sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (SOS) occurs in 3–10% of patients post-transplant, characterized by tender hepatomegaly, ascites, jaundice, and fluid retention. It peaks at day 16 and has a mortality rate of ~30%. Defibrotide treatment can reduce mortality.
keyPoints: SOS incidence: 3–10% of patients.
Peak occurrence at day 16 post-transplant.
Mortality rate: ~30% without defibrotide.
Defibrotide reduces mortality.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.title: Therapeutic Approach to Transplant-Associated Thrombotic Microangiopathy
items.content: Transplant-associated thrombotic microangiopathy, seen in 5–10% of patients about a month post-transplant, is characterized by schistocytes on smear, elevated lactate dehydrogenase, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury. Changing immunosuppressive regimens can be effective, with some response to eculizumab.
keyPoints: Incidence: 5–10% of patients.
Symptoms: schistocytes, elevated lactate dehydrogenase, thrombocytopenia, acute kidney injury.
Treatment: change in immunosuppressive regimen, eculizumab response.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.title: Understanding and Managing Graft Failure
items.content: Graft failure post-transplant can occur due to inadequate stem cell numbers, myelotoxic agents, or immunologic rejection. In autologous transplants, issues may arise from ex vivo damage or myelotoxic exposure. Allogeneic graft failures often involve T-cell-mediated rejection and are more likely with less immunosuppressive regimens or HLA mismatches.
keyPoints: Graft failure can result from inadequate stem cell numbers, ex vivo damage, or myelotoxic agents post-transplant.
Immunologic rejection in allogeneic transplants is often T-cell-mediated and more common with less immunosuppressive regimens or HLA mismatches.
Treatment involves removing myelotoxic agents and a trial of myeloid growth factors; reinfusion without additional immunosuppression is usually unsuccessful.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.title: Identifying and Treating Acute Graft-Versus-Host Disease
items.content: Acute GVHD typically occurs within the first 3 months post-transplant, peaking around 4 weeks. It presents with skin rashes, gastrointestinal symptoms, and liver dysfunction. Diagnosis often requires biopsy to confirm lymphocytic infiltrates and endothelial damage. Grades II to IV require aggressive treatment due to significant morbidity.
keyPoints: Acute GVHD occurs within the first 3 months post-transplant, peaking around 4 weeks.
Symptoms include erythematous rash, anorexia/diarrhea, and liver dysfunction with elevated bilirubin and enzymes.
Diagnosis often requires biopsy; grades II to IV require aggressive treatment.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.title: Preventing Acute GVHD: Strategies and Challenges
items.content: Standard GVHD prevention includes calcineurin inhibitors and antimetabolites, with added benefits from a third agent like mycophenolate mofetil. Despite prophylaxis, ~30% of matched sibling recipients develop acute GVHD. Risk factors include HLA mismatches, patient/donor age, intense regimens, and multiparous female donors.
keyPoints: Standard prevention involves calcineurin inhibitors and antimetabolites; adding a third agent can be beneficial.
~30% of matched sibling recipients develop acute GVHD despite prophylaxis.
Risk factors for acute GVHD include HLA mismatches, patient/donor age, intense regimens, and multiparous female donors.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.title: Chronic GVHD: Incidence, Management, and Mortality
items.content: Chronic graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) is a common complication following allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation, occurring in 20–50% of recipients. It typically manifests between 3 months to 2 years post-transplantation and resembles autoimmune disorders with symptoms like malar rash and sicca syndrome. Mild cases may be managed locally with topical treatments, while severe cases require systemic therapy such as prednisone, which is effective in 40–60% of patients.
keyPoints: Chronic GVHD occurs in 20–50% of allogeneic transplant recipients.
Typically develops between 3 months and 2 years post-transplant.
Mortality rates range from 5 to 50%, averaging around 15%.
Systemic therapy usually involves prednisone, effective in 40–60% of patients.
FDA-approved treatments for steroid-resistant chronic GVHD include ibrutinib, ruxolitinib, and belumosudil.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 3)

items.title: Autoimmune Disorders Post-Transplant: Incidence and Treatment
items.content: Post-transplant, 3 to 5% of patients may develop autoimmune disorders such as autoimmune hemolytic anemia or idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura. These conditions are more common in recipients with unrelated donor sources and chronic GVHD but can occur without obvious GVHD. Treatment typically involves prednisone, cyclosporine, or rituximab.
keyPoints: 3 to 5% of patients develop autoimmune disorders post-transplant.
Common disorders include autoimmune hemolytic anemia and idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura.
Risk factors: unrelated donor source and chronic GVHD.
Treatment options: prednisone, cyclosporine, or rituximab.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 3)

items.title: Infection Management Post-Transplant
items.content: Post-transplant patients are at high risk for infections, particularly in the early neutropenic phase. Prophylactic measures include broad-spectrum antibiotics when granulocyte counts fall below 500/μL and antifungal agents like fluconazole or voriconazole for higher-risk patients. CMV prevention is achieved through seronegative blood products or preemptive therapy with drugs like letermovir. Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia can be prevented with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, and respiratory viruses are managed by avoiding contact with infected individuals and using neuraminidase inhibitors for influenza.
keyPoints: Broad-spectrum antibiotics initiated when granulocyte count <500/μL.
Prophylaxis against fungal infections improves survival; fluconazole or voriconazole used based on risk.
CMV prevention with letermovir or preemptive therapy for seropositive patients.
Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia prevented with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole.
Respiratory viruses managed by avoiding contact and using neuraminidase inhibitors.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 3)

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 4) | Nonmalignant Diseases | IMMUNODEFICIENCY DISORDERS | APLASTIC ANEMIA
items.title: Timing and Prophylaxis for Infections Post-Transplantation
items.content: Infection risk significantly decreases after 3 months post-transplant unless chronic GVHD necessitates ongoing immunosuppression. Prophylactic measures, including trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole during immunosuppressive therapy and acyclovir for varicella-zoster for one year, are recommended to mitigate infection risks.
keyPoints: Infection risk diminishes beyond 3 months post-transplant unless chronic GVHD is present.
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis should continue while on immunosuppressive drugs.
Acyclovir for varicella-zoster recommended for one year after transplant.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 4) | Nonmalignant Diseases | IMMUNODEFICIENCY DISORDERS | APLASTIC ANEMIA

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 4) | Nonmalignant Diseases | IMMUNODEFICIENCY DISORDERS | APLASTIC ANEMIA
items.title: Efficacy of Transplantation in Severe Aplastic Anemia
items.content: Transplantation from matched siblings using a regimen of high-dose cyclophosphamide and antithymocyte globulin cures over 95% of patients under 40 with severe aplastic anemia. Improved outcomes have led to recommendations for transplantation as initial therapy, even in older patients or those receiving mismatched transplants.
keyPoints: >95% cure rate in patients <40 years old with matched sibling transplant using high-dose cyclophosphamide and antithymocyte globulin.
Transplantation is recommended as initial therapy due to improved outcomes, even for older patients or those receiving mismatched transplants.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 4) | Nonmalignant Diseases | IMMUNODEFICIENCY DISORDERS | APLASTIC ANEMIA

items.title: Curative Potential of Marrow Transplantation in Thalassemia Major and Sickle Cell Anemia
items.content: Marrow transplantation from an HLA-identical sibling using a preparative regimen of busulfan and cyclophosphamide can cure 80–90% of patients with thalassemia major, especially if performed before complications like hepatomegaly or portal fibrosis develop. For sickle cell anemia, matched sibling or cord blood transplantation shows two-year survival and disease-free survival rates of 95% and 85%, respectively.
keyPoints: 80–90% cure rate for thalassemia major with HLA-identical sibling marrow transplant
Best outcomes if transplanted before hepatomegaly or portal fibrosis, and after adequate iron chelation therapy
5-year survival and disease-free survival rates are 95% and 90%, respectively, in thalassemia patients
Two-year survival and disease-free survival rates of 95% and 85%, respectively, for sickle cell anemia with matched sibling or cord blood transplant
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · HEMOGLOBINOPATHIES | OTHER NONMALIGNANT DISEASES

items.title: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation in Nonmalignant Diseases and Autoimmune Disorders
items.content: Transplantation can cure congenital disorders of white blood cells (e.g., Kostmann's syndrome, chronic granulomatous disease) and certain anemias (e.g., Blackfan-Diamond anemia). It is also used for storage diseases like Gaucher's disease and autoimmune disorders such as severe scleroderma, with improved event-free and overall survival noted in a prospective randomized trial.
keyPoints: Cures congenital disorders of white blood cells (e.g., Kostmann's syndrome, chronic granulomatous disease)
Cures certain anemias like Blackfan-Diamond anemia
Used for storage diseases such as Gaucher's disease; early treatment increases success chances
Improved event-free and overall survival in severe scleroderma with hematopoietic cell transplantation
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · HEMOGLOBINOPATHIES | OTHER NONMALIGNANT DISEASES

items.title: Role of Allogeneic Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation in AML
items.content: Allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation is a curative option for ~30% of AML patients who do not achieve complete response after induction chemotherapy. It offers the highest cure rates when performed during first remission, with long-term disease-free survival averaging 55–60%. The best outcomes are seen in second remission or first relapse, where cure rates reach 45-50%. Transplantation shows a survival advantage over chemotherapy for patients under 60 years with adverse and intermediate-risk AML.
keyPoints: Cures ~30% of non-responsive AML patients post-induction chemotherapy
Long-term disease-free survival in first remission: 55–60%
Cure rates in second remission or first relapse: 45-50%
Survival advantage over chemotherapy for <60 years with adverse/intermediate-risk AML
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · ACUTE LEUKEMIA

items.title: Transplantation Strategy in Acute Lymphocytic Leukemia (ALL)
items.content: In adults with ALL, allogeneic transplantation can cure ~30% of patients who do not achieve complete response to induction chemotherapy. Cure rates improve to 40-50% when performed in second remission. Transplantation during first remission results in a 65% cure rate. MRD-negative assessments before transplantation enhance outcomes. Allogeneic transplantation offers a survival advantage over chemotherapy for high-risk patients, while autologous transplantation is not recommended due to higher relapse rates.
keyPoints: Cures ~30% of non-responsive ALL adults post-induction chemotherapy
Cure rates in second remission: 40-50%
First remission cure rate: 65%
Survival advantage for high-risk patients over chemotherapy
Autologous transplantation not recommended due to higher relapse rates
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · ACUTE LEUKEMIA

items.title: Indications and Cure Rates for Allogeneic Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation in CML
items.content: Allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation is recommended for patients with chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) who have failed therapy with two or more tyrosine kinase inhibitors during the chronic phase, offering cure rates of 70%. It is also advised for those progressing to accelerated phase or blast crisis, despite lower cure rates.
keyPoints: Indicated for CML patients in chronic phase after failure of ≥2 tyrosine kinase inhibitors
Cure rate of 70% expected in such cases
Recommended for accelerated phase or blast crisis with lower cure rates
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CHRONIC LEUKEMIA | MYELODYSPLASIA AND MYELOPROLIFERATIVE DISORDERS | LYMPHOMA

items.title: Criteria and Outcomes for Allogeneic Transplantation in Myelodysplasia
items.content: Allogeneic transplantation can cure 40-65% of myelodysplasia patients, particularly younger ones or those with less advanced disease. It is generally reserved for patients with an IPSS score of Int-2 or higher, or selected Int-1 cases with poor prognostics.
keyPoints: Cure rate of 40-65% in myelodysplasia
Better outcomes in younger patients and less advanced disease
Transplantation typically for IPSS score Int-2+ or selected Int-1 with poor prognostics
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CHRONIC LEUKEMIA | MYELODYSPLASIA AND MYELOPROLIFERATIVE DISORDERS | LYMPHOMA

items.title: Role of Autologous Transplantation and Maintenance Therapy in Myeloma
items.content: Autologous transplantation as part of initial therapy for myeloma improves disease-free survival and overall survival. Lenalidomide maintenance therapy post-transplant further enhances outcomes.
keyPoints: Prospective randomized studies show improved disease-free survival and overall survival with autologous transplantation in first-line therapy.
Lenalidomide maintenance therapy following transplantation provides additional benefit.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · MYELOMA | SOLID TUMORS | POSTTRANSPLANT RELAPSE

items.title: Treatment Options for Post-Transplant Relapse in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia and Other Conditions
items.content: Patients relapsing after autologous transplantation may respond to further chemotherapy or allogeneic transplantation. Donor lymphocyte infusions can induce complete responses, with varying success rates depending on the condition.
keyPoints: Complete response rates: 75% in chronic myeloid leukemia, 40% in myelodysplasia, 25% in AML, and 15% in myeloma with donor lymphocyte infusions.
Complications include transient myelosuppression and GVHD, influenced by the dose and schedule of lymphocyte infusions.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · MYELOMA | SOLID TUMORS | POSTTRANSPLANT RELAPSE

items.subtopic: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation | THE HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL
items.question: Which term is preferred for describing the collection and transplantation of hematopoietic stem cells?
items.explanation: The preferred term is 'hematopoietic cell transplantation' due to the use of various sources like peripheral blood and umbilical cord blood.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation | THE HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL

items.subtopic: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation | THE HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL
items.question: What allows hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) to be cryopreserved?
options: Their ability to home to the marrow space
The production of CXCL12 by the niche stroma
Their ability to survive freezing and thawing with little damage
items.explanation: Hematopoietic stem cells can be cryopreserved because they can survive freezing and thawing with little, if any, damage.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation | THE HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL

items.subtopic: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation | THE HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL
items.question: What is a key feature of hematopoietic stem cells that facilitates their transplantation?
options: The interaction between P- and E-selectins on marrow sinusoidal endothelium with integrins
Their production by the niche stroma
items.explanation: A key feature is their ability to home to the marrow space, involving interactions between P- and E-selectins on marrow sinusoidal endothelium with integrins.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation | THE HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL

items.question: Which type of hematopoietic cell transplantation involves no risk of graft-versus-host disease (GVHD)?
items.explanation: Syngeneic donors, such as identical twins, eliminate the risk of GVHD.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.question: What is the probability that a patient has an HLA-identical sibling if they have three siblings?
items.explanation: The probability is calculated as $1 - (0.75)^n$, where n=3, resulting in 87.5%.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.question: What is the risk of severe acute GVHD following transplantation between HLA-identical siblings?
items.explanation: The risk of severe, life-threatening acute GVHD is approximately 15%.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.question: What is a disadvantage of autologous transplantation compared to allogeneic transplantation?
options: Lack of GVT effect and potential tumor cell contamination
items.explanation: Autologous transplantation lacks a graft-versus-tumor effect and can be contaminated with tumor cells.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.question: Which agent is used to increase the mobilization of CD34+ cells when growth factor alone is insufficient?
items.explanation: Plerixafor, an antagonist of CXCR4, is used to increase the mobilization of CD34+ cells when growth factor alone is insufficient.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.question: What is a common advantage of using peripheral blood stem cells over autologous marrow for transplantation?
items.explanation: Peripheral blood stem cells result in more rapid hematopoietic recovery compared to autologous marrow.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.question: What is a disadvantage of using peripheral blood stem cells for allogeneic transplantation with matched unrelated donors?
items.explanation: The use of peripheral blood stem cells in allogeneic transplantation with matched unrelated donors results in more chronic GVHD without a compensatory survival advantage.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.question: What is one of the advantages of using cord blood for stem cell transplantation?
items.explanation: Cord blood has decreased immune reactivity, allowing for the use of partially matched units.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.question: Which preparative regimen is typically sufficient for aplastic anemia to allow marrow graft acceptance?
items.explanation: High-dose cyclophosphamide plus antithymocyte globulin are sufficient to immunosuppress the patient adequately for aplastic anemia.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · THE TRANSPLANT PREPARATIVE REGIMEN

items.question: What is a common component of regimens used to treat malignant diseases?
options: Busulfan, cyclophosphamide, melphalan, thiotepa, carmustine, etoposide, and total-body irradiation
items.explanation: Regimens for malignant diseases commonly include busulfan, cyclophosphamide, melphalan, thiotepa, carmustine, etoposide, and total-body irradiation.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · THE TRANSPLANT PREPARATIVE REGIMEN

items.question: What is the primary reason for using reduced-intensity conditioning regimens in older patients or those with significant comorbidities?
options: To reduce toxicity and lower transplant-related mortality
items.explanation: Reduced-intensity regimens are used to reduce toxicity and lower transplant-related mortality, which is favorable for older patients or those with significant comorbidities.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · THE TRANSPLANT PREPARATIVE REGIMEN

items.question: Which site is typically used for collecting marrow from a donor?
items.explanation: Marrow is usually collected from the donor's posterior and sometimes anterior iliac crests.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · THE TRANSPLANT PROCEDURE

items.question: What is a common reason for further processing of collected marrow in ABO-incompatible transplants?
options: Removal of red cells to prevent hemolysis
items.explanation: The removal of red cells is done to prevent hemolysis in ABO-incompatible transplants.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · THE TRANSPLANT PROCEDURE

items.question: What symptom might patients experience due to the taste and smell of cryoprotectant used during stem cell infusion?
items.explanation: Patients sometimes experience short-lived nausea or vomiting due to the taste and smell of dimethyl sulfoxide, a cryoprotectant.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · THE TRANSPLANT PROCEDURE

items.question: Which agent is used to prevent hemorrhagic cystitis caused by high-dose cyclophosphamide?
items.explanation: High-dose cyclophosphamide can result in hemorrhagic cystitis, which can usually be prevented by bladder irrigation or with the sulfhydryl compound mercaptoethanesulfonate (MESNA).
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.question: What is a common symptom of sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (SOS) following hematopoietic cell transplantation?
items.explanation: Sinusoidal obstruction syndrome of the liver results in symptoms such as tender hepatomegaly, ascites, jaundice, and fluid retention.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.question: What is a characteristic finding on biopsy in patients with diffuse interstitial pneumonia due to direct toxicity after transplant?
options: Collagen and granulation tissue around bronchial structures
items.explanation: Biopsies in cases of diffuse interstitial pneumonia due to direct toxicity are typically characterized by diffuse alveolar damage.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.question: Which chronic pulmonary disease occurring after hematopoietic cell transplantation is characterized by dry cough and a diffuse, fluffy infiltrate on chest imaging?
items.explanation: Cryptogenic organizing pneumonia is a restrictive lung disease characterized by dry cough, shortness of breath, and chest imaging showing a diffuse, fluffy infiltrate.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common cause of graft failure after autologous transplantation?
options: Inadequate numbers of stem cells being transplanted
Damage during ex vivo treatment or storage
Exposure to myelotoxic agents after transplant
Immunologic rejection by immunocompetent host cells
items.explanation: Immunologic rejection is a cause of graft failure in allogeneic transplantation, not autologous.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.question: What is commonly associated with the loss of marrow function after hematopoietic cell transplantation?
options: Infections with cytomegalovirus (CMV) or human herpesvirus type 6
Use of full-dose drugs to prevent GVHD
Reinfusion of donor stem cells without immunosuppression
Presence of host lymphocytes in allogeneic transplant recipients
items.explanation: CMV or human herpesvirus type 6 infections have been associated with loss of marrow function.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.question: What is the recommended approach if lymphocytes of host origin persist in allogeneic transplant recipients with graft failure?
options: Immediate reinfusion of donor stem cells
Administering a second immunosuppressive preparative regimen before reinfusion
Increasing doses of myeloid growth factors
items.explanation: Reinfusion is usually unsuccessful without preceding it with a second immunosuppressive regimen.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.question: Which factor does NOT increase the risk of acute GVHD?
options: Use of less intense preparative regimens
Use of multiparous women as donors
items.explanation: Less intense preparative regimens are associated with a lower risk of acute GVHD.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following drugs have received U.S. Food and Drug Administration approval for the treatment of steroid-resistant chronic GVHD?
items.explanation: Ibrutinib, ruxolitinib, and belumosudil are kinase inhibitors approved for steroid-resistant chronic GVHD.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 3)

items.question: What prophylactic treatment is recommended to prevent Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia in post-transplant patients?
items.explanation: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is used for prophylaxis against Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following agents is effective as prophylaxis for CMV in seronegative patients transplanted from seronegative donors?
items.explanation: Letermovir is effective as prophylaxis for CMV in seronegative patients transplanted from seronegative donors.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 3)

items.question: What is the most common cause of infection during the period from engraftment until about 3 months after transplant?
items.explanation: Gram-positive bacteria, fungi (particularly Aspergillus), and viruses including CMV are common causes of infection during this period.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 3)

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 4) | Nonmalignant Diseases | IMMUNODEFICIENCY DISORDERS | APLASTIC ANEMIA
items.question: Which of the following is recommended for prophylaxis against varicella-zoster after hematopoietic cell transplantation?
items.explanation: Acyclovir is recommended for prophylaxis against varicella-zoster for 1 year after transplant.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 4) | Nonmalignant Diseases | IMMUNODEFICIENCY DISORDERS | APLASTIC ANEMIA

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 4) | Nonmalignant Diseases | IMMUNODEFICIENCY DISORDERS | APLASTIC ANEMIA
items.question: When should vaccination against SAR-CoV-2 begin after hematopoietic cell transplantation?
items.explanation: Vaccination against SAR-CoV-2 begins at 3 months after transplantation.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 4) | Nonmalignant Diseases | IMMUNODEFICIENCY DISORDERS | APLASTIC ANEMIA

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 4) | Nonmalignant Diseases | IMMUNODEFICIENCY DISORDERS | APLASTIC ANEMIA
items.question: What is the cure rate for severe aplastic anemia in patients under age 40 years following transplantation from matched siblings?
options: Cure rates are not significantly affected by patient age
items.explanation: Transplantation cures >95% of patients under age 40 years with severe aplastic anemia.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 4) | Nonmalignant Diseases | IMMUNODEFICIENCY DISORDERS | APLASTIC ANEMIA

items.question: What is the reported probability of 5-year survival following marrow transplantation from an HLA-identical sibling for patients with thalassemia major?
items.explanation: The passage states that the probability of 5-year survival is 95%.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · HEMOGLOBINOPATHIES | OTHER NONMALIGNANT DISEASES

items.question: For which condition is hematopoietic cell transplantation potentially curative, according to the passage?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that transplantation is potentially curative for thalassemia major.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · HEMOGLOBINOPATHIES | OTHER NONMALIGNANT DISEASES

items.question: What are the reported two-year survival and disease-free survival rates following matched sibling or cord blood transplantation in patients with sickle cell anemia?
items.explanation: The passage reports two-year survival and disease-free survival rates of 95% and 85%, respectively.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · HEMOGLOBINOPATHIES | OTHER NONMALIGNANT DISEASES

items.question: What is the approximate cure rate for patients with acute myeloid leukemia (AML) who undergo allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation during first remission?
items.explanation: The best results with allogeneic transplantation for AML are achieved when applied during first remission, with long-term disease-free survival rates averaging 55–60%.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · ACUTE LEUKEMIA

items.question: In adults with acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL), what is the recommended use of agents like blinatumomab before transplantation?
options: To achieve an MRD-negative state in first remission.
To increase overall survival rates in second remission.
To decrease nonrelapse mortality in high-risk patients.
To ensure a complete response to induction chemotherapy.
items.explanation: Use of agents such as blinatumomab is recommended to achieve an MRD-negative state before transplantation, improving transplant outcomes when carried out with negative MRD assessments in second remission.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · ACUTE LEUKEMIA

items.question: What is the role of autologous hematopoietic cell transplantation in the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (AML) according to the passage?
options: It offers a survival advantage over chemotherapy.
It is recommended for patients with favorable-risk AML.
It is used primarily in first remission.
items.explanation: Autologous transplantation has no defined role in the treatment of AML as stated in the passage.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · ACUTE LEUKEMIA

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LEUKEMIA | MYELODYSPLASIA AND MYELOPROLIFERATIVE DISORDERS | LYMPHOMA
items.question: In patients with chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) who have failed therapy with two or more tyrosine kinase inhibitors, what is the expected cure rate following allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation?
items.explanation: Cure rates of 70% can be expected in such patients.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CHRONIC LEUKEMIA | MYELODYSPLASIA AND MYELOPROLIFERATIVE DISORDERS | LYMPHOMA

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LEUKEMIA | MYELODYSPLASIA AND MYELOPROLIFERATIVE DISORDERS | LYMPHOMA
items.question: Which factor is NOT a reason for the limited study of allogeneic transplantation in chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL)?
options: The chronic nature of the disease
High cure rates with conventional therapy
items.explanation: The passage does not mention high cure rates with conventional therapy as a reason for limited study.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CHRONIC LEUKEMIA | MYELODYSPLASIA AND MYELOPROLIFERATIVE DISORDERS | LYMPHOMA

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LEUKEMIA | MYELODYSPLASIA AND MYELOPROLIFERATIVE DISORDERS | LYMPHOMA
items.question: For which patients is allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation generally reserved in myelodysplasia?
options: Patients with an IPSS score of Int-2 or higher, or selected patients with an IPSS score of Int-1 with poor prognostic features
Patients who can live extended periods without intervention
items.explanation: Transplantation is generally reserved for patients with an IPSS score of Int-2 or higher, or selected patients with an IPSS score of Int-1 who have other poor prognostic features.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CHRONIC LEUKEMIA | MYELODYSPLASIA AND MYELOPROLIFERATIVE DISORDERS | LYMPHOMA

items.question: Which therapy has been shown to improve disease-free survival and overall survival in myeloma patients when included as part of initial treatment?
items.explanation: Prospective randomized studies demonstrate that the inclusion of autologous transplantation as part of initial therapy results in improved disease-free survival and overall survival.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · MYELOMA | SOLID TUMORS | POSTTRANSPLANT RELAPSE

items.question: What is a potential outcome for patients with testicular cancer after failing first-line platinum-containing chemotherapy?
options: Cure in ~25% of cases with low-dose salvage chemotherapy
No chance of cure regardless of treatment
Cure in ~50% of cases with high-dose chemotherapy and autologous stem cell support
items.explanation: Patients with testicular cancer can still be cured in ~50% of cases if treated with high-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell support.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · MYELOMA | SOLID TUMORS | POSTTRANSPLANT RELAPSE

items.question: What is a major complication associated with donor lymphocyte infusions?
items.explanation: Major complications of donor lymphocyte infusions include transient myelosuppression and the development of GVHD.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · MYELOMA | SOLID TUMORS | POSTTRANSPLANT RELAPSE

items.subtopic: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation | THE HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL
items.statement: Transplantation of a single stem cell can replace the entire lymphohematopoietic system in humans.
items.explanation: In humans, transplantation requires a small percentage of donor bone marrow volume for complete replacement.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation | THE HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL

items.subtopic: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation | THE HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL
items.statement: The frequency of hematopoietic cell transplants is closely associated with gross national income (GNI) per capita.
items.explanation: There is a close association between transplant rates and GNI per capita, although differences exist among countries.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation | THE HEMATOPOIETIC STEM CELL

items.statement: The risk of graft rejection is higher when using family member donors mismatched for one, two, or three antigens.
items.explanation: Mismatching increases the risk of graft rejection and GVHD.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.statement: HLA-matched unrelated donors are identified for all patients who need a transplant.
items.explanation: HLA-matched donors can be found for ~60% of patients, not all.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.statement: Peripheral blood stem cell transplantation results in improved survival compared to autologous marrow.
items.explanation: No studies show improved survival with peripheral blood stem cells over autologous marrow.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.statement: Use of double cord transplants reduces the risk of graft failure and early mortality.
items.explanation: Double cord transplants diminish the risk of graft failure and early mortality even though only one donor ultimately engrafts.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.statement: Nonmyeloablative regimens are the minimum required to achieve engraftment and cause only transient myelosuppression if no transplant were performed.
items.explanation: Nonmyeloablative regimens, such as fludarabine plus 200 cGy total-body irradiation, are minimal for engraftment and would cause only transient myelosuppression without transplantation.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · THE TRANSPLANT PREPARATIVE REGIMEN

items.statement: High-dose regimens are preferred for patients with significant comorbidities.
items.explanation: Reduced-intensity conditioning is favored in older patients and those with significant comorbidities due to lower transplant-related mortality.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · THE TRANSPLANT PREPARATIVE REGIMEN

items.statement: Marrow donation is associated with frequent complications.
items.explanation: Marrow donation is safe, with only very rare complications reported.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · THE TRANSPLANT PROCEDURE

items.statement: Peripheral blood counts reach their nadir immediately after transplant due to the preparative regimen.
items.explanation: Peripheral blood counts reach their nadir several days to a week after transplant.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · THE TRANSPLANT PROCEDURE

items.statement: Sinusoidal obstruction syndrome typically develops within the first week after transplant.
items.explanation: Symptoms of sinusoidal obstruction syndrome can develop any time during the first month after transplant, with peak incidence at day 16.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.statement: Most patients will experience complete reversal of bronchiolitis obliterans.
items.explanation: Complete reversal of bronchiolitis obliterans is uncommon, although it may respond to increasing immunosuppression.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.statement: Grade I acute GVHD usually requires aggressive therapy.
items.explanation: Grade I acute GVHD is of little clinical significance and does not require treatment.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.statement: Disruption of the intestinal microbiota is associated with a higher risk of GVHD.
items.explanation: Loss of diversity and overgrowth by a single taxon in the microbiota increases GVHD risk.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.statement: Patients with chronic GVHD should receive prophylactic trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole.
items.explanation: Chronic GVHD patients are susceptible to significant infection and should receive prophylactic trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 3)

items.statement: Late-onset acute GVHD requires different treatment from classic acute or chronic GVHD.
items.explanation: No data suggest that patients with late-onset acute GVHD should be treated differently than those with classic acute or chronic GVHD.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 3)

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 4) | Nonmalignant Diseases | IMMUNODEFICIENCY DISORDERS | APLASTIC ANEMIA
items.statement: Live vaccines are generally administered before 2 years after hematopoietic cell transplantation.
items.explanation: Live vaccines are generally not administered before 2 years post-transplant.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 4) | Nonmalignant Diseases | IMMUNODEFICIENCY DISORDERS | APLASTIC ANEMIA

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 4) | Nonmalignant Diseases | IMMUNODEFICIENCY DISORDERS | APLASTIC ANEMIA
items.statement: Patients with Fanconi's anemia require less intensive preparative regimens due to their sensitivity.
items.explanation: Fanconi's anemia patients are sensitive to alkylating agents, necessitating less intensive preparative regimens.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 4) | Nonmalignant Diseases | IMMUNODEFICIENCY DISORDERS | APLASTIC ANEMIA

items.statement: Hematopoietic cell transplantation is the only curative treatment for thalassemia.
items.explanation: The passage states that although prolonged survival can be achieved with aggressive chelation therapy, transplantation is the only curative treatment.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · HEMOGLOBINOPATHIES | OTHER NONMALIGNANT DISEASES

items.statement: Hematopoietic cell transplantation has been uniformly successful in treating storage diseases such as Gaucher's disease.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that transplantation for these diseases has not been uniformly successful.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · HEMOGLOBINOPATHIES | OTHER NONMALIGNANT DISEASES

items.statement: Prospective trials comparing hematopoietic cell transplantation with chemotherapy for older patients are lacking.
items.explanation: The passage states that prospective trials comparing hematopoietic cell transplantation with chemotherapy are lacking for older patients.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · ACUTE LEUKEMIA

items.statement: Allogeneic transplantation is the only form of therapy that can cure AML patients who do not achieve a complete response after induction chemotherapy.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation cures ~30% of such patients and is the only form of therapy that can cure them.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · ACUTE LEUKEMIA

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LEUKEMIA | MYELODYSPLASIA AND MYELOPROLIFERATIVE DISORDERS | LYMPHOMA
items.statement: Allogeneic transplantation can cure patients with primary myelofibrosis or myelofibrosis secondary to polycythemia vera or essential thrombocythemia, with 5-year progression-free survival rates in excess of 65%.
items.explanation: The passage states that allogeneic transplantation can cure these conditions with reported 5-year progression-free survival rates in excess of 65%.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CHRONIC LEUKEMIA | MYELODYSPLASIA AND MYELOPROLIFERATIVE DISORDERS | LYMPHOMA

items.subtopic: CHRONIC LEUKEMIA | MYELODYSPLASIA AND MYELOPROLIFERATIVE DISORDERS | LYMPHOMA
items.statement: Autologous transplantation is generally preferred over allogeneic transplantation for patients with intermediate- or high-grade non-Hodgkin's lymphoma due to fewer complications and equivalent survival.
items.explanation: Most experts favor autologous rather than allogeneic transplantation because it results in fewer complications and appears to have equivalent survival.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CHRONIC LEUKEMIA | MYELODYSPLASIA AND MYELOPROLIFERATIVE DISORDERS | LYMPHOMA

items.statement: High-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell support is being studied for several solid tumors, including neuroblastoma and pediatric sarcomas.
items.explanation: The passage states that high-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell support is being studied for several other solid tumors, including neuroblastoma and pediatric sarcomas.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · MYELOMA | SOLID TUMORS | POSTTRANSPLANT RELAPSE

items.statement: Patients who relapse following allogeneic transplantation have no further treatment options.
items.explanation: Several options are available for patients who relapse following allogeneic transplantation, including treatment with infusions of unirradiated donor lymphocytes.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · MYELOMA | SOLID TUMORS | POSTTRANSPLANT RELAPSE

items.assertion: The use of posttransplant high-dose cyclophosphamide has diminished the impact of HLA mismatching.
items.reason: This allows transplantation between donor/recipient pairs who share only one HLA haplotype.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.assertion: Allogeneic transplantation can be performed across ABO blood barriers.
items.reason: This is achieved by removing isoagglutinin and/or incompatible red blood cells from the donor graft.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.assertion: Peripheral blood stem cells lead to more rapid hematopoietic recovery.
items.reason: The use of peripheral blood stem cells results in faster engraftment than marrow.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.assertion: The use of cord blood results in a higher incidence of GVHD.
items.reason: Cord blood contains a high number of T cells.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because cord blood results in a lower incidence of GVHD, and the reason is also false as it reflects the low number of T cells in cord blood.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · CATEGORIES OF HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.assertion: Adaptive immunity may take 1–2 years to fully recover after hematopoietic cell transplantation.
items.reason: Survival and peripheral expansion of infused donor T cells is the dominant mechanism for T-cell recovery in the first months.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why adaptive immunity takes 1–2 years to recover.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · THE TRANSPLANT PROCEDURE

items.assertion: Use of a patient-controlled analgesic pump provides the greatest patient satisfaction for managing oral mucositis.
items.reason: It results in a lower cumulative dose of narcotic.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the use of a patient-controlled analgesic pump provides greater patient satisfaction.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.assertion: Thyroid dysfunction is always severe and requires treatment after hematopoietic cell transplantation.
items.reason: Thyroid dysfunction, usually well compensated, is sometimes seen post-transplant.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because thyroid dysfunction is usually well compensated. The reason is true as it correctly states that thyroid dysfunction can occur but is often not severe.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 1)

items.assertion: Acute GVHD is characterized by an erythematous maculopapular rash.
items.reason: Endothelial damage and lymphocytic infiltrates are seen in organs affected by acute GVHD.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain the rash.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.assertion: Standard high-dose preparative regimens can be tolerated well if administered within 100 days of a first transplant.
items.reason: Cumulative toxicities make standard high-dose regimens poorly tolerated within this timeframe.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; these regimens are poorly tolerated due to cumulative toxicities.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 2)

items.assertion: Chronic GVHD can resemble an autoimmune disorder.
items.reason: It presents with symptoms such as malar rash, sicca syndrome, arthritis, obliterative bronchiolitis, and bile duct degeneration with cholestasis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why chronic GVHD resembles an autoimmune disorder.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 3)

items.assertion: Patients with late-onset acute GVHD should be treated differently than those with classic acute or chronic GVHD.
items.reason: There is no data suggesting a different treatment approach for late-onset acute GVHD.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true; there's no evidence to suggest a different treatment for late-onset acute GVHD.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · COMPLICATIONS FOLLOWING HEMATOPOIETIC CELL TRANSPLANTATION (part 3)

items.assertion: Hematopoietic cell transplantation can cure congenital disorders of white blood cells.
items.reason: Transplantation has been used successfully to treat conditions like Kostmann's syndrome and chronic granulomatous disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · HEMOGLOBINOPATHIES | OTHER NONMALIGNANT DISEASES

items.assertion: The best results for high-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell support are obtained in patients with limited amounts of disease.
items.reason: These patients have remaining tumors that remain sensitive to conventional-dose chemotherapy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for why best results are seen in these patients.
items.reference: Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation · MYELOMA | SOLID TUMORS | POSTTRANSPLANT RELAPSE
